sections.summary: Starting amiodarone in a patient on digoxin roughly doubles digoxin serum concentrations, creating a substantial risk of digoxin toxicity unless the dose is preemptively reduced.
sections.mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein-mediated renal and biliary clearance of digoxin, and it also reduces digoxin's volume of distribution, both of which raise serum digoxin levels. Amiodarone's long half-life means this effect can take weeks to fully develop.
sections.clinical_effects: Digoxin levels commonly increase by 70-100%, producing anorexia, nausea, visual disturbances, and cardiac arrhythmias including heart block if the toxicity goes unrecognized.
sections.management: Reduce the digoxin dose by approximately half when amiodarone is started, and recheck levels before making further adjustments; consider empirically lowering the dose again if amiodarone therapy continues long term.
sections.monitoring: Obtain a baseline digoxin level before starting amiodarone and recheck at 1-2 weeks and again at steady state, watching for anorexia, nausea, or visual symptoms suggestive of toxicity.

evidence.citation: Nademanee K, et al. Am Heart J. 1984;108(4):895-899
